instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: (Some slides are from Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2013)

images.ai_description: University of Pittsburgh logo featuring a stylized shield with blue and yellow elements, accompanied by the university name in blue text.

images.ai_description: A diagram illustrating system architecture: a user-level application interfaces with the kernel's file system and device driver, connecting to hardware components like storage.
This image features a vintage computer keyboard with a classic layout, gray keys, and a numeric keypad, representing early personal computing hardware design.
This diagram illustrates the layers of an I/O system, detailing processes from hardware operations to user processes, including drivers, interrupt handlers, and software functions.

text_blocks.text: Programs can access any I/O device

runs.text: Programs can access any I/O device

text_blocks.text: No need to specify device in advance

runs.text: No need to specify device in advance

runs.text: Name of a file or device is a string or an integer

text_blocks.text: Doesn’t depend on the machine (underlying hardware)

runs.text: Doesn’t depend on the machine (underlying hardware)

text_blocks.text: Done as close to the hardware as possible

text_blocks.text: Data coming off a device cannot be stored in final destination

text_blocks.text: Up to two surfaces per platter

runs.text: Up to two surfaces per platter

text_blocks.text: One or more platters per disk

runs.text: One or more platters per disk

images.ai_description: Gray, circular, flat 3D disc, resembling a button or coin, with a smooth surface. Suitable for illustrating concepts in geometry, design, or technology.
A flat, round, gray disc with a smooth surface, possibly representing a platform or base; suitable for illustrating concepts in geometry or physics.
A 3D-rendered, dark gray oval disc with a smooth surface and subtle gradient shading, creating a sleek, modern appearance, ideal for design discussions.
Hard disk diagram showing multiple platters with tracks. A read/write head accesses data across cylindrically aligned tracks. Illustration emphasizes disk structure and data retrieval.
Diagram illustrating hard disk components: a platter with concentric tracks (red) and subdivided sectors (blue), showcasing data storage organization in computing.

text_blocks.text: • Transfer time = time to rotate over sector

runs.text: • Transfer time = time to rotate over sector

text_blocks.text: • Seek time dominates, with rotation time close

runs.text: • Seek time dominates, with rotation time close

text_blocks.text: • Error checking is done by controllers

runs.text: • Error checking is done by controllers

images.ai_description: Formulas explaining disk storage: sectors per track, tracks per cylinder, and bytes per sector. Visualizes calculating total disk capacity in bytes by combining equations.

images.ai_description: A sketched cylinder highlights a sector with red arrows pointing to it, emphasizing focus or importance. The handwritten label reads "sector."
Binary data and error detection are shown with even parity examples. Illustrations include parity bits and checks for potential errors in transmission.
The image explains error detection with binary data and ECC, illustrating even-parity and odd-parity methods using crossed-out symbols and annotated sections.
Hamming code illustration: 7-bit data with parity for error detection and correction. Highlights detecting 2-bit errors and correcting 1-bit errors, including a cube visualization.

text_blocks.text: ECC helps with errors, but sometimes this isn’t enough

text_blocks.text: Disks keep spare sectors (normally unused) and remap bad

runs.text: Disks keep spare sectors (normally unused) and remap bad

text_blocks.text: If there’s time, the whole track could be reordered…

runs.text: If there’s time, the whole track could be reordered…

images.ai_description: Three circular diagrams illustrating disk sector management: (a) shows bad sectors and spares, (b) depicts a replacement sector in use, and (c) a standard configuration.
